sections.heading: Defining the Problem
sections.body: Problem formulation is the step of translating a real-world task into a precise structure a search algorithm can work with — deciding exactly what counts as a state, what actions are available, and what it means to succeed, before any searching begins.

sections.heading: Initial State
sections.body: Formulating a problem starts by defining the initial state precisely — the exact starting configuration, described using only the details relevant to solving the problem, since including irrelevant detail makes the state space unnecessarily large.

sections.heading: Goal Test
sections.body: The goal test determines whether a given state counts as a solution. It can be as simple as checking equality against one specific target state, or a more general check (e.g. "all items are sorted") that many different states could satisfy.

sections.heading: Actions
sections.body: Defining the problem requires specifying the set of actions available at each state — this is often expressed as a function that, given a state, returns the set of actions that can legally be applied from it.

sections.heading: Path Cost
sections.body: The path cost function assigns a numeric cost to a sequence of actions, typically as the sum of individual step costs. This lets a search algorithm compare different solution paths and prefer cheaper ones, not just any path that happens to reach the goal.

sections.heading: Constraints
sections.body: Constraints are conditions that limit which states or actions are valid — for example, a delivery route problem might constrain routes to only use roads a truck is legally allowed on. Correctly capturing constraints during formulation prevents the search from wasting time on, or returning, invalid solutions.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of problem formulation?
options: To skip defining the problem and start searching immediately
To translate a real-world task into a precise structure (states, actions, goal) a search algorithm can use
To guarantee the fastest possible hardware
To eliminate the need for a goal test

quiz.question: The goal test is used to:
options: Determine whether a given state counts as a solution

quiz.question: Why does path cost matter during problem formulation?
options: It lets the search compare solution paths and prefer cheaper ones, not just any goal-reaching path
It replaces the need for a goal test
